items.title: Hobart's Southern Outlet widening project hit by $32m blowout

children.content: The cost of widening Hobart's Southern Outlet has blown out by another $32 million, with completion of the northern section of the project now estimated at $95 million.

image.altText: Southern Outlet Hobart

items.title: Potential students allege visa scam tied to education consultancy

children.content: Aspiring students looking to study abroad in Australia say they've been left blindsided and with their futures in doubt after an education consultancy promised them migration and visa advice.

image.altText: A graphic pictures a thoughtful woman and text reading 'Visa expiring soon? Discuss your options'.

items.title: Marinus will break energy 'deadlock' hobbling Tasmania, economist says

children.content: An economist says Marinus Link remains an investment in the national interest and will shore up Tasmania's energy security. It follows a report last week questioning the project's business case.

image.altText: An artists rendition of a large cable coming out of a blue ocean.

items.title: Nude swimmers take chilly plunge in Dark Mofo solstice swim

children.content: Dark Mofo's Nude Solstice Swim marks the end of Tasmania's fortnight-long winter arts festival.

image.altText: Crowd of swimmers prepare to enter the water for Dark Mofo's 2026 Nude Solstice Swim

items.title: Tasmanian logs made their way to Victoria. Here's what we found when we gave chase

children.content: Four Corners follows the logs, and the money, to uncover how efforts to end logging have shifted the timber trade across state borders.

image.altText: A photo taken through a windshield of a truck with logs on it driving on a road.

gallery.altText: A photo shot through the windscreen of a car, showing a truck loaded with logs ahead on a semi-suburban street.

thumbnail.altText: A photo shot through the windscreen of a car, showing a truck loaded with logs ahead on a semi-suburban street.

items.title: Photo gives clues to missing man's final hours before disappearance

children.content: A previously unreleased image answers one question in the case of missing Queensland man Jack McLennan, but many others remain.

gallery.altText: Jack McLennan jumps into Barambah Creek hours before he vanished.

thumbnail.altText: Jack McLennan jumps into Barambah Creek hours before he vanished.

items.title: Security agencies issue blunt 'act swiftly' warning on AI

image.altText: A row of men in suits sitting on a stage

children.content: The Five Eyes agencies warn leaders must "act swiftly" to address the imminent threat as AI increases the "speed, scale and sophistication of cyber threats".

items.title: Iran to allow nuclear inspections, JD Vance says

children.content: The US vice president says that progress has been made in talks with Iran, with Tehran agreeing to allow nuclear inspectors and a mechanism to keep the Strait of Hormuz open.

image.altText: A close-up image of a man with short brown hair and blue eyes, pointing with his index finger as he looks up

items.title: Will SpaceX bring stocks thudding back to earth?

children.content: As we plough on through the fourth consecutive year of global stock records and huge annual advances, it's not surprising that many pundits have begun to question just how much longer this boom can continue.

image.altText: A white rocket launches into the sky on a clear day, with grey and yellow clouds billowing below.

items.title: Can reflecting the Sun buy time for net zero? A startup has raised $US75m to try

children.content: Private companies want to reflect the Sun to cool the planet. But scientists fear the geoengineering technology could disrupt monsoon rains a billion people depend on.

gallery.altText: the earth rotates and is surrounded by tiny reflective particles

items.title: Australian trust in China grows as belief in US hits record low

children.content: The Lowy Institute's latest poll shows Australia's trust in the US has fallen to a record low.

items.title: MPs to knock off early for war memorial extension opening

children.content: Federal parliament is set to adjourn early today so politicians can attend the official opening of a hall as part of the controversial $550 million extension of the War Memorial.

items.title: Australia secures biggest-ever defence export deal with Canada

children.content: Canada is buying a powerful radar system to monitor the Arctic, modelling the system on Australia's JORN radar.

image.altText: Richard Marles radar Canada

items.title: The Rosetta Stone was taken from Egypt. This artist took it back

children.content: The Rosetta Stone changed the way we understood Ancient Egypt. It sits in the British Museum but this artist wanted to return it home. To pull it off he needed some legal advice, a digital scanner and the courage to take on the way we look at history.

items.title: Quiz: Do you know a lychee from a rambutan? Name these fruits

children.content: Can you tell these tropical fruits apart? It's not as easy as you think.

items.title: Second Australian case of deadly bird flu confirmed

children.content: Authorities say a second bird found sick on WA's south coast had the deadly H5 strain of avian flu, as one of the nation's biggest chicken producers announces it is locking down its WA operations.

image.altText: Giant Northern Petrel

items.title: What we know about how H5N1 bird flu got here and where it may appear next

children.content: Authorities were worried about avian influenza coming in from the north, until the virus started moving across the Southern Ocean. Now infected birds have been found in Western Australia. Where else could birds carrying the virus land?

image.altText: A portrait side-on view of a brown gull-like bird with a black beak.

items.title: Quick hits: Iran's incredible keeper and young Yamal starts in style

children.content: Young Lamine Yamal scored less than 10 minutes into his World Cup debut; Iran and Cabo Verde both held out for famous draws against highly-fancied opponents Belgium and Uruguay respectively; and Cabo Verde's most famous mum arrives to see her son. Here's the quick hits from the World Cup.

articles.title: Vegemite levels help Socceroos' chef find out if players like his food

children.content: Chef Vini Capovilla has learnt a thing or two catering for the Socceroos at four World Cups. And one is finding out if they like his food.

image.altText: The Socceroos have a team barista in the hotel

articles.title: The most culturally diverse World Cup ever meets Trump's America

children.content: Fans from all over the world are being welcomed by locals across North America to celebrate the World Cup. But can it make a lasting difference in the US, where President Donald Trump has cracked down on immigration?

image.altText: A group of Haiti football fans cheer inside the stadium

articles.title: Egypt claims first ever World Cup win at New Zealand's expense

children.content: Mo Salah's goal helped Egypt to a 3-1, come from behind victory over New Zealand, while Cabo Verde held Uruguay to a stunning 2-2 draw in Miami for yet another magical moment.

image.altText: Mohamed Salah leaps in the air

items.title: Tax system favours older Australians over younger, report finds

children.content: Australia's tax policy settings are leading to vastly different outcomes for people who have the same gross incomes but are simply different ages, a new report shows.

image.altText: A woman with an open laptop puts her head in her hands

items.title: Japan to increase entry visa fees by more than five times current amount

children.content: Japan will quintuple entry visa fees starting from July 1, but most Australians will not be affected.

items.title: The moment Tehran's men leave JD Vance waiting in a Swiss negotiating room

children.content: In a room in a glamorous Swiss resort, the United States delegation, Pakistani and Qatari officials were awaiting the arrival of the Iranian representatives. But as Tehran's men enter the room, they appear to notice the press.

items.title: Operator of troubled Callide power station sued for more than $1b

children.content: Foreign investment group Sev.en said a series of catastrophic failures at the Callide power station had led to 1,700 days of lost generation capacity.

items.title: How gambling companies 'conduct psychological experiments' on their customers

items.title: Tony Modra's family 'extremely grateful' for support after crash

items.title: Gus Lamont's grandmother denies involvement in disappearance
